claim: Demographics are the single most underpriced secular force across asset classes.
reason: Self-contradictory. The cited evidence (JGB yields, Chinese equity deratings, Korean KOSPI stagnation) itself demonstrates that markets have already priced demographics. The claim is unfalsifiable as stated.

claim: Demographics will add 50–150bp to equilibrium inflation.
reason: Contradicted by the empirical base rate across every demographic slowdown analogue. Japan (30 years of deflation/low inflation), Germany, and Korea all experienced disinflation during demographic deceleration. The Goodhart-Pradhan thesis has a 0-for-3 empirical track record.

claim: EM credits in India/ASEAN offer 'demographic-adjusted relative value' vs. DM peers.
reason: Asserted without evidence that demographic tailwinds are under-reflected in spreads. EM spreads embed governance, institutional, currency, and convertibility risk that are not demographic discounts. Demographic dividends convert to credit improvement only through functioning institutions. No mechanism specified.

claim: AI productivity gains could render dependency ratio arithmetic largely irrelevant within a decade.
reason: Rhetorical overreach. Actual measured US productivity growth (1.5–2.0% in 2023–2025) is only modestly above pre-COVID trend despite massive AI investment. Rendering dependency ratios largely irrelevant within a decade requires productivity growth rates never sustained outside wartime.

claim: Mainstream fiscal sustainability projections (e.g., CBO) are theoretically incoherent (at 9/10 confidence).
reason: The logical critique of applying household budget constraints to currency issuers has merit, but the 9/10 confidence conflates theoretical validity with practical relevance. CBO projections serve a specific institutional purpose and drive actual legislation. Most cited aging economies (Italy, Germany, France) are not independent currency issuers. Downgraded to 5/10.

claim: Fiscal multipliers in aging societies are structurally lower.
reason: Self-undermining. The same agent argues aging economies hit the ZLB more frequently, where multipliers are large (Christiano et al. 2011). The demographic composition effect (lower MPC among retirees) and ZLB frequency effect (higher multipliers at ZLB) have opposing signs, making the net effect ambiguous, not clearly negative.

claim: Bond yield compression in demographic slowdowns shows a 4/4 base rate (at 8/10 confidence).
reason: Conflates demographics with confounding variables. Germany 2005–2015 was dominated by Eurozone sovereign debt crisis and ECB QE. Italy 2000–2010 reflects ECB policy and euro convergence. The isolated demographic contribution cannot be extracted with precision. Should be 5–6/10 for the demographic component alone.
